item.title: Genealogy of Poker
item.description:
Poker descends from several distinct game traditions that contributed different mechanical elements. No single ancestor game contains all of poker’s features. Poker is a composite.

Worked example: five-card draw and Texas hold’em

Compare five-card draw with Texas hold’em. In five-card draw, each player begins with a complete hidden hand and may replace some cards before the final betting round. In Texas hold’em, each player begins with only two private cards and later reasons from a shared public board.

item.title: History of Poker
item.description:
Poker’s history is a sequence of transformations in which a simple betting card game absorbed new mechanics, migrated across social contexts, and eventually became both a professional discipline and a mass spectator activity.

Worked example: 1970 to 2003

In 1970, the first World Series of Poker was a small invitational gathering at Binion’s Horseshoe in Las Vegas [@wsop2026about; @wsop2026history]. In 2003, Chris Moneymaker won the WSOP Main Event after qualifying online, and the game’s audience exploded [@wsop2026history]. Those two moments show the scale of poker’s historical shift: from a small in-person gambling culture to a global spectator and online competition.

item.title: Poker Overview
item.description:
Audience: readers familiar with card games and gambling basics.

Learning goal: describe poker’s core structure and what distinguishes it from other gambling games.

Worked example: winning without showdown

In a simple no-limit Texas hold’em hand, five players receive two private cards each. One player raises before the flop. Everyone else folds. The raiser wins the pot immediately without showing cards.

This is the fastest way to see what makes poker structurally different from many other gambling games. The winning player may or may not have held the best hand. It does not matter, because the hand ended before showdown. Poker therefore rewards not only card strength but also the ability to represent strength credibly enough that opponents give up [@sklansky1999].
